Sentence: You wake with the taste of wet soil at the back of your teeth and the soft bend of the world still asleep beneath condensation.
The tablet has been flat against your ribs all night; the
battery icon is sleepily generous.
You flex your fingers and the cold of the metal clips to your palm the same way the salt in the air clings to the underside of your jacket.
You sit up.
The rooftop smells like turned earth and sea—peat-sweet on one breath, metallic ozone on the next.
Your feet find the worn planks without looking.
Somewhere below, someone drags a crate across a balcony; somewhere beyond the glass of the city, breakers are rehearsing their long, patient roar.
You fingers trace the tide line engraved on the brass pendant at your throat.
The mark is a neat, ruled notch—an insistence you learned to read before you learned to persuade.
The pendant is cool and
this is not only data.
People sleep under the waterline you model.
• Roll over and try to get five more minutes
You pull the edge of your jacket up to your chin and close your eyes.
The rooftop hum keeps pace with your breathing—two slow, even counts—and the idea of the council room is a distant, dim shape.
• Sit up and stand, let the cold make you sharp
You push your knees under you and stand.
The chill breathes through the jumpsuit and your shoulders unclench.
The tablet's glow is a horizon you need to cross.
You choose—unconsciously—to stand.
A shadow moves in the mist.
Emil Kwon steps out past the trellis, his shirt darkened with early-morning damp, a crate of seedlings in one arm and a sheaf of paper rolled in the other.
The
mist has made his hair more tousled than usual, ocean-soft at the edges.
He takes in the rooftop with the same small, practical appreciation he gives to anything that could be fixed with hands and steady
people.
Morning.
(He smiles without the wide show—it arrives as a small, steady thing.) 'You look like you haven't slept.'
I ran the model again.
'uncertainty.' It sounds thinner when you say it aloud.
(He sets the crate down and lifts a seed tray, letting the wet green steam a moment.) Emil Kwon Got seedlings.
Mangrove mixes and some marsh grasses; thought we might fold them into the floating beds this week.' (He taps the rolled papers.) 'And, uh—made a quick retrofit sketch.
Low-tech floatation, modular so folks can build a piece at a time.
You said you were going to the promenade later.
There is an easy pause while the irrigation hums and a gull calls.
You look at his palm where a corner of the sketch peeks out—hand-drawn lines, neat but impatient.
The kind of drawing that wants to be built before it's perfectly drawn.
• Take the sketch and examine it now
You take the papers and let the sketch unfurl.
Your thumbs trace a thin marker line, counting densities and attachment points like reading a small, honest map.
It's clever—simple redundancies where the city over-engineers—and it smells faintly of coffee.
• Pocket the sketch and pull up the tablet instead
You fold your fingers around the tablet and the screen wakes.
Rows of numbers and color bands swim an honest blue.
The sketch waits in Emil's hand like a promise.
The crate of seedlings settles between you; the trellis throws soft, patterned shadows across its soil.
You make a decision without announcing it—you can do both, you think, but one thing you know with the quiet
your world has always leaned on the way numbers can make the city move.
Asha’s name flashes across the tablet first in the stubborn little icon that has gotten you out of bed more times than you can count.
You tap, and her voice spills from a voicemail like warm tea poured too fast—tart, immediate.
Asha (voicemail) Maya—you're up.
Council wants you to present this afternoon.
I left notes.
They'll push for 'bold numbers.' Don't cave to the spectacle—make them accountable to the block maps.
Call me when you have a minute.
You listen to it twice.
The phrase 'bold numbers' sits in your chest with the soft density of fog.
Boldness in a council room often means rounding up the certainty; it means a tidy headline and
an uneasy community.
Asha's voice has the light grit of someone who has watched marshlands be traded for parking lots and still believes you can make them ask for better.
(You say aloud, to nowhere and to the rooftop at once.) 'They want bold numbers.'
(He leans against a post and watches you, as if counting the steadiness of your shoulders.) Emil Kwon Bold like spectacle, or bold like… responsible?
That's the problem.
(You run a thumb along the tide-line on your pendant.
The metal is warm from the night's sleep against your skin.) 'Bold can mean bulldozers and a concrete solution that protects some at the expense of others.
Bold can mean taking the easy headline, not the hard conversation.'
(His face tightens, but only enough to register worry.) Emil Kwon So what do you want, Maya?
Quietly right or loudly wrong?
You look down.
Guilty faces sit on the back of your eyelids—the low-lying block you couldn't save, the salt-stiff sheets on a night the pumps failed.
The memory doesn't scream; it simply sits with the same
steady weight as the pendant.
Responsibility has a way of feeling like a ledger that can never balance.
A text bubbles up—Asha again, terse this time Council wants bold numbers.
You flip the tablet.
New flood-projection data populates the screen in neat gradients—cobalt, pewter, pale washed green.
The bottom line is not a single number but a ledger of possible tides and timelines, each with its
own polite uncertainty.
The software has given you many versions of 'what if.' You have trained yourself to love what-ifs; they are where compromise breathes.
They want certainty.
(You hear the tilt in your voice; the map is not kinder for your reading.) 'And we don't have it.
Not in their terms.'
Then show them the terms that matter.' He slides a seedling free and holds it out to you—small, bellied root and oily leaf.
'Show them the beds we can build without waiting for permits.
Bring a human scale to the numbers.
Let them smell the wet soil.
You want to argue, to draw a line between polices and practice—to tell him that the models shape the money and the money shapes what gets built.
But his hands are steady; his enthusiasm is a
build with us, build now.
There's an intimacy in him offering a living thing at a time like this.
It is not romantic theater—it is practical faith.
We can patch parts of the promenade this week.
Diego's got volunteers.
People will come if they can see something tangible.
(You rub your thumb over the pendant again.) 'If I go in with small fixes, the council will ask why the city isn't doing the big thing.
They'll point to a single seawall and call it decisive.'
(He shrugs, the motion casual, but a shadow passes his mouth.) 'Maybe so.
Maybe they need to see both.'
seedlings that will root in marsh and tide; a hand-sketched retrofit promising dignity and agency; a tablet full of plausible futures.
The rooftop is an island of warmth and green
in a city that is both wet and brittle.
Light pools in the gutters of leaves.
The hum of irrigation is a metronome for small acts of care.
• Tell Emil you'll present a mixed proposal—living shorelines plus targeted defenses
You form the words slowly, weighing each one against the map on your tablet.
'I'll lead with a hybrid,' you say.
Emil nods, quiet approval in his eyes.
The seedling between you seems to lean toward the sun.
• Tell Emil you'll present the model as-is, let the numbers speak
You say, 'I'll stick with the model.' It feels antiseptic when it leaves your mouth—clean, uncluttered.
Emil exhales a small, private sound that could be disappointment or acceptance.
He doesn't press.
You do not pick either path yet.
You hold both in the same hand like different shells from the same shore.
Your internal clock—used to late nights and early briefings—winds itself into a gentle preparedness.
The arousal of the morning stays low; there is no rush, only the patient building of small truths.
It feels like a good morning to be careful.
Asha's voicemail pulses in the corner of the tablet one more time They'll want you at the council in the afternoon.
Be ready for questions about displacement.
tides, wind, public meetings.
The rooftop gives you a thin, necessary perspective—soil, seed, and the people who will plant them.
(You fold the tablet and tuck it into your jacket.) 'I'll come in,' you tell Emil.
'But I need your retrofit drawings and Diego's group at the promenade to bring a team.
We show them both the map and the hands.'
I'll be there.
(He pauses, then adds, quieter.) 'And Maya—don't forget to breathe between the numbers.' He smiles without theatricality, a small, human punctuation.
You allow yourself one steady inhale.
The pendant presses warm against your skin.
The seedlings in their crate seem to settle into a collective hush.
Clouds remain low; the breakers continue their soft rehearsal.
The morning
the council room that waits like a question.
A last glance at the Tideward Promenade planned in your head—stilted stalls, patched tarpaulin, hands rebuilding what the sea tries to unmake.
You imagine the seedlings there, green and insistently alive where tides meet human stubbornness.
You step toward the ladder and the stairwell down, the crate still between you two.
The rooftop's lights sway in a tiny, wet wind.
You have models, seedlings, and Asha's voice.
The morning could tilt a conversation into policy or keep it tied to people.
Which will you put in front of the council?
You step inside and the air shifts—ozone and coffee, the faint, wet tang of algae pressed into sample vials.
The lab's glass walls catch the bay as a line of gray; inside, screens throw a cool
wash across your face, making the silver streak in your hair shine like a margin note.
Your tablet is tucked under your arm.
Your palms still know the weight of the crate from the rooftop, though
it's gone from your hands and not from your head.
delicate lines of mangrove root analogs, sediment-trap sketches, time-series graphs that bulge and flatten like breath.
You run your thumb along a simulated cross-section until the cursor blinks
back at you—data in neat columns, community-reported damage layered into the model as qualitatively as any sensor reading.
Asha stands near the lab bench, bent over a petri of marsh grass, spectacles low.
When she looks up the room pauses with her—she smells like cold marsh and eucalyptus tea, and there's a steadiness in the set of her shoulders that steadies you in return.
They'll be frayed at the edges today,' she says.
'Remember—numbers are anchors, not weights.
If you hand us a rope, they won't drown.
You try to answer with method instead of sentiment.
You show her the composite map—wave attenuation here, community access preserved there.
You say the words you trained to say attenuation coefficients,' 'cost-benefit adjusted for displacement,' 'phased co-design.
Good,' she says, then softens.
'Speak the technical.
But don't let it be all ledger and no people.
You nod, though your throat tightens.
You want to make the room see marshland as civic fabric, not as a checkbox on an environmental assessment.
• Re-run the simulation one more time
You slide a new data layer into the model—community-reported damage from the last storm—watching the attenuation curves adjust slowly.
It calms you in the way familiar routines do.
• Close the laptop and rehearse your opening aloud
less perfect, more human.
Asha watches the brief ritual—your fingers, your breath.
She does not tell you to stop calibrating.
Instead she puts a hand on the back of a stool, the smallest steadiness, and says nothing about your tendency to keep polishing.
You let the lab be a place to collect yourself.
Diego's messages ping at the edge of your tablet—short bursts, schematic thumbs—reminders that the promenade will be there regardless of how the council votes.
You tuck them away like small coals you might later warm.
You lift your tablet, the models backed up and annotated.
You move toward the sliding doors with the small, measured gait of someone trying not to carry the world into a room that is already crowded with expectations.
The Council Hall smells different—less of earth, more of varnish and old paper, the tang of everyone in a closed room holding their own opinions like coins.
Mayor Lila Chen sits at the dais, plant brooch
glinting, a measured smile that keeps public weather from breaking into private storms.
Her attentive face is practiced at holding different voices at once.
At the back of the room, half in shadow, Noah Ortega stands with his arms folded, tablet balanced in one hand.
There is a line at his temple, a kind of focus that has nothing to
contained, perhaps weary, definitely calculating.
You cannot tell if he has already decided, or is still
assembling reasons to persuade himself.
You ready, Maya?
He sets a small retrofit prototype on the table before you—modest, handmade, honest.
It smells faintly of tar and wet rope.
people can make things that work without a billion-dollar bond underwritten by promises.
I am,' you say.
'I have the attenuation models and the community-phase plan.
I will be concise.
We appreciate clarity.' She gestures toward the lectern.
'When you're ready, Ms.
Reyes.
You step forward, lift the tablet so the blue of the monitors casts you in that lab-light again, and begin.
Council members, Mayor Chen—I've prepared a suite of living-shoreline scenarios that reduce nearshore wave energy by up to thirty percent in pilot zones, while preserving access and local economy.
They work with existing marshes, require smaller capital outlays over five years, and—' (you slow) '—they allow communities to remain active partners in the maintenance.
deliberate, cool, and trying not to rush.
Noah Ortega stands then, interrupted not by heat but by the movement of inevitability.
He sets down a sleek schematic of a reinforced seawall—an engineered arc, clean as a promise.
The Consortium has modeled a contiguous seawall that protects the economic core immediately,' he says.
'Bonding mechanisms are ready.
Timelines are compressed to reduce exposure during the high-risk window next year.
That design gives protection in concentrated zones, but it also accelerates displacement risk for lower-lying neighborhoods.
Floodwaters don't respect municipal boundaries—they shift pressure.
Our surveys show disproportionate impact on cultural centers if the wall is built at scale without participatory measures.
We have to choose what we save.
A distributed shoreline slows erosion but loses time.
A seawall buys us predictive certainty while we transition infrastructure for the rest.
Your pulse softens into a low ache.
You can see the logic on his tablet—numbers that glitter with authority—but between his words and your data is a ledger that lists more than monetary loss.
It lists memory, markets, food stalls, the rhythm of Sundays on stilts.
Mayor Lila Chen (interjecting, diplomatic) Both proposals have merit.
What's the projected municipal expenditure difference, Ms.
Reyes?
Upfront capital favors the seawall.
Life-cycle costs and social externalities favor the living-shoreline approach when you factor in avoided displacement and ecosystem services.
The question isn't only money—it's who builds and who decides.
Diego leans forward, voice threaded with the urgency of someone who distributes flyers in the rain.
We can build the living-shoreline solutions with community workshops.
People will show up when they know they have agency.
We don't need to be done by market quarters—we need to be done with who will live there.
And what if the process isn't done in time?
Markets don't wait for consensus.
If the core collapses, the entire municipal revenue stream collapses with it.
That threatens services for everyone you want to protect.
You feel each argument like tide forces against a seawall of will.
The room listens, the air between words holding small, electric fractures.
Conversations begin to cross; a council aide whispers numbers, a member frowns at the spreadsheet, a reporter's pen scratches.
• Underline community quotes on your slides before Q&A
You swipe through your deck, pinning a slide with testimonies from promenade vendors.
The room shifts; some faces soften, others look for the ledger beneath the human words.
• Leave the slides data-focused and answer the human questions orally
You close the testimony slide and prepare to answer questions with stories rather than static pages.
When asked, you will have to conjure faces and places out of memory—and that makes your voice rawer, but real.
(after a beat) 'Ms.
Reyes, please speak to cost and timeline specifics.'
(after a beat) 'Ms.
Reyes, please speak to cost and timeline specifics.'
Phased implementation—pilot in the promenade and adjacent neighborhoods in year one, monitoring-driven scale-up in years two and three—keeps costs staggered and allows for community labor inputs.
We can lower capital demands by leveraging grassroots workshops and local procurement.
Council Member But will it physically stand up to the projected seven-year storm events?
We are hearing from engineers that these are non-trivial risks.
The models show targeted attenuation in pilot zones reduces immediate wave power on key piers and market structures.
We'll combine engineered anchors at strategic nodes—surgically placed—so this isn't an either/or.
The risk isn't eliminated, but the social risk of displacement is mitigated.
Noah Ortega (leaning in, quietly) Mitigation comes with an asterisk when budgets are tight and bond markets demand guarantees.
Hybrid approaches are politically delicate.
Who guarantees the guarantees?
You hear it like a question that wants more than an answer.
Guarantees require institutions.
Institutions, in turn, require power.
You think of Diego rolling up maps in damp hands, Emil Kwon's practical optimism, Asha's softly practical insistence that evidence and people are not mutually exclusive.
Asha appears then, not from behind you but beside the dais, her movement smooth and patient.
She pulls you aside, her hand on your elbow an anchor you accept without thinking.
Her breath smells of marsh again.
You can hold both evidence and people,' she says, very close, so only you hear.
'Do not let the glint of a single solution blind you to the slow work.
I'm trying not to,' you whisper.
'But they—' (you gesture toward the room, toward Noah Ortega, toward the spreadsheet—too many things) '—they hear numbers louder than voices.
Then give them both.
Numbers that reference faces, and faces who can point to process.
Make them uncomfortable in a way that also makes them responsible.
You step back into the light and continue to answer the threaded questions.
The pace is measured, not frantic, each response a small, deliberate wave rather than a breaking surge.
You find that the low hum
in the room is not a lull but a pressure—the kind best felt slowly, seeping into the joints.
bond yields forecast,
rapid construction schedules, contingency clauses.
They glitter the way polished metal does in low sun—beautiful enough to distract.
When the session breaks for a short recess, the hall's energy feels tilted—the Consortium's numbers have moved from background noise to a visible weight on the table.
The council members murmur in small groups; the futures of neighborhoods are weighed in whispered calculus.
You fold your tablet, heavier than when you arrived.
It is not only the data but the knowledge that the room's appetite is for clear, fast solutions.
That appetite is not wrong—it is frightened—but it does not automatically listen for the slow voices.
Emil Kwon (quietly, at your shoulder) Whatever happens, we'll keep building.
If they vote seawall, we adapt our designs.
If they don't, we scale what we can.
I know.
I just… I don't want to go back to feeling like every decision erases someone.
Then tell them that.
Tell them what gets erased.
You step toward the exit with the small prototype tucked back into Emil Kwon's satchel.
Asha's hand squeezes your shoulder—brief, a punctuation.
patient, assessing, like a tide you cannot yet predict.
It is not hostility as much as a current—quiet, inevitable, and demanding accounting.
You have given them evidence and voices.
The room's tilt is measurable now; the next move will feel like the city choosing between speed and the slow tending of community life.
There are votes
to be cast, leaks to be feared, and alliances to be tested.
Whatever comes next, the tablet in your bag is heavier with consequence than with data.
You fold the tablet again because your hands need the motion.
The device feels heavier than when you closed it in the council chamber; the weight is not silicon and glass but the shape of decisions
a consortium amendment, language sharpened like a blade—accelerated timelines, “asset reset” phrasing folded into legalese, clauses that could justify relocating entire
low-lying blocks under the guise of efficiency.
Your brass tide-line pendant presses warm against your sternum as if trying to point you away from the shore.
It doesn't.
It spins, not with the ease of a compass but with a frantic insistence that makes your chest tighten.
You read the sentences again in the dark.
Each line feels like a measurement you already failed to take expedited procurement,' 'foreseeable asset consolidation,' 'contingent eminent domain.
Memory comes like cold spray.
You are back on a gravel street with sandbags in your arms, the night a sheaf of rain, the generator's breath rasping in the alley.
Neighbors—names you have not said aloud
in months—calling for help you couldn't give fast enough.
A child's small hand under a soaked blanket.
You had mapped the storm then and failed to turn the curve.
The guilt is a tide you wear.
Maya.
Listen—meet me at the Old Beacon.
Bring nothing but yourself.
Do not let this go quiet.
Her tone is the worn cord in a bell—urgent but composed.
Emil Kwon's name, then Diego's group chat lighting up with a photo you already know will be everywhere—Noah Ortega at a press podium, lapel pin gleaming, headlines framing the
emergency session called.
A city that smells of wet rope and antiseptic is turning toward a single, sharp decision.
You stand.
The room is small and smells of damp paper and coffee cooling at the rim.
Outside the promenade is a river of market lights and tarps, water kissing pilings like a repetitive question.
Emil Kwon is there before you fully cross the main boardwalk.
He stands with his hands thrust into the pockets of his patched canvas, shoulders squared as if bracing for a wind that isn't only meteorological.
Sea-glass eyes find you and hold—wet, rimmed in the same gray as the sky, but steady.
You saw it.
He doesn't wait for a reply; his voice is a low current.
You nod.
You don't have to say the words; the amendment lives under the skin of the morning.
The clauses are worse than the rumor.
They don't just speed things up—they re-title assets under contingency.
It's a legal shutter.
bold letters—'NO DISPLACEMENT.' His face is an open map of contained anger.
They'll call it triage.
They'll call it efficient.
They'll call the people 'collateral.' I won't let them call our neighbors that.
He exhales, a small, fierce sound.
Diego Ramos materializes from beneath a canopy, armfuls of flyers, rain beading on his shoulders.
He breathes in and out like a man used to shouting over crowds.
We got the leak out on the boards an hour ago.
People are pissed.
People are scared.
If the vote goes like that, my cousin's block—gone.
Told them to meet at the community hall tonight.
He spreads a map with trembling fingers.
pins like bruises on a city you love.
Mayor Chen called an emergency session.
Noah Ortega's face is already in the headlines with that pin.
You make a small gesture, half laugh, half curse.
'He's framing it as necessary protection.'
Of course he is.
It's what he does—sells the idea of safety while clearing the decks.
He looks down at his hands, then up at you.
'Maya, what do you want to do?'
negotiate amendments at the council table, stand visibly with Emil Kwon and the makers in street resistance, or dig for the consortium's
strings and expose them.
Each is a different kind of exposure—public bargaining, public defiance, or surgical public unmasking.
Diego Ramos leans in, voice low and urgent.
We can stand at the Hall tonight.
Make it loud.
Make them see the faces.
If people show up—if we break their veneer—maybe they stall.
He slams a damp palm on the map.
'We have to make it political, not just technical.'
Your mind runs through protocol, calibrations, projections.
Negotiations can be precise; they can also make compromises that look like protections and feel like betrayals.
Resistance can hold people together, but can it stop bulldozers and bonds?
Investigation can root out rot, but it takes time—time the council says they don't have.
• Hand out flyers with Diego
You slip into the crowd, the paper cool and wet in your palm.
Diego nods and hands you a stack; people take them with hands that shake.
Your voice—when you raise it—is steady, brittle with anger, and someone meets your eyes and doesn't look away.
• Call Asha and tell her you'll meet at the Old Beacon
You dial fast.
Asha Karim answers on the second ring.
You leave your location terse and exact; she replies with a sharp promise to wait—her 'do not let this go quiet' hangs between you.
The call closes with the sound of her coat zipper.
• Confront Noah publicly on his feed — post the amendment and name the clause
the excerpt, the image of the clause.
Notifications explode — some supportive, some accusing you of theatrics.
Noah Ortega's office issues a denial within the hour.
The pin in the photos gleams under studio lights.
• Promise to negotiate from inside—tell them you'll talk to the Mayor
You pull out your tablet and draft a terse message to the Mayor's office.
You mention amendments, safeguards.
Emil Kwon watches you—his jaw softens but his eyes narrow with worry.
• Stand with Emil and pledge to be at the Hall tonight
You reach across the damp table and grip Emil Kwon's sleeve.
Your voice is low, thick.
He smiles a small, broken smile and pulls you close for an instant—enough to promise not comfort, but solidarity.
a line of people working with their hands instead of waiting for a council room to decide for them.
The promenade answers you in noise and motion—hands knotting rope, a chorus of instruction rising and falling like the tide.
You step between platforms, the boards underfoot slick with sea-spray and sawdust.
Salt tangs the air; the smell of wet canvas and hot solder mixes with coffee from a makeshift thermos.
Your jacket rubs against the
brass pendant at your throat; its tide line seems to nod.
You feel both steadiness and a tightening—an energy like a wound pulled taut, ready to release.
elbow-deep under a pontoon frame, then up on a ladder measuring a keel, then back to retying a tarpaulin line.
His hands move like someone who’s read the water and
learned how to make something hold.
When he looks up, his sea-glass eyes find you.
He doesn't wait for drama; he hands you a roll of laminated posters without pausing.
We made the tide-alert stickers smaller so people can put them on their satchels.
Diego hacked the API so it flashes red on low batteries.
Good.
Make the map simplified—neighborhood names, safe assembly points, a QR for the petition.
No jargon.
No charts.
You sound like you came from the lab and then decided to be a carpenter.
Someone has to translate the lab into something people can afford to follow.
Then let's make it touch skin.
And if someone trips over a rope tonight, I'm taking them home.
The banter keeps the edge from sharpening into panic.
But Diego cuts across with a megaphone and a list.
Block A will take pontoons; Block B will handle gardens; Block C does outreach.
Volunteers at 18:00 for assembly.
We hold the slated staging site at first light—no one moves the equipment while we're there.
And the human chain?
Dawn.
We get a line from the eastern pilings to the staging site.
Social ops pushes the story tonight.
Diego’s voice is firm, the kind that organizes people into a thing that can make noise and resist being invisible.
You watch him hand out gloves, label them with neighborhood names, and one of the younger
volunteers stumbles, cheeks bright with exertion and a laugh.
The scene is messy and honest—exactly what you wanted.
You set up a livestream with Emil Kwon kneeling beside you, the model balancing on a milk crate.
Your voice steadies as soon as words find shape.
Neighbors—this is what a living shoreline looks like in practice.
These beds don't just bloom—they slow surge, they give people time.
They keep stalls standing.
They're made with what we can build together.
They need this.
Another: Don't let them take the staging site.
The feed is small-scale democracy made visible, but it hums.
In the taped corner of your tablet, a notification blinks from
the Council—Noah Ortega's office—an official request to explain the occupation plan publicly tomorrow.
The needle of time shifts; the bandwidth for mistakes narrows.
You look wind-worn.
I know.
I'm trying to turn risk into plans.
Plans are good.
Telling your people why they matter is better.
Make them feel the tether—not just the threat.
Emil is talking in knots; Diego is talking in routes; I have the maps.
I need them to feel the same urgency without hearing panic.
Then tell them how to be useful.
People can handle urgency if it's handed as a tool.
Emil Kwon, overhearing, glances over and says, 'Or hand them a hammer.' He grins, but his eyes are sharper when he says, 'We can do the staging site tonight.
The pontoons are almost ready.
If we can keep the Consortium machines off the barge line, we keep the site visible.'
Your chest tightens at 'machines.' The word conjures cranes, contracts signed behind polished doors, and crews who will clear a line in the night if no one watches.
Noah will call us idealists.
Let him.
Labels are cheap.
People are not.
Mayor Lila Chen walking the waterfront with a consultant, her expression folded
The Mayor worries about timelines.
You watch the clip three times.
Lila's mouth is steady.
Her voice in the background—muted on the video—says something about 'coordination' and 'avoiding chaos.' You imagine the Council chamber, fluorescent and hungry for order.
Your mind
the Consortium accelerates permit filings; security is contracted; a press release frames you as obstructionists.
Your internal calculus sharpens into tasks.
Translate the lab data into stories.
Keep the staging site occupied.
Build a narrative people care to share.
focus the livestream on the garden demo
You angle the camera tighter on the model, your voice lower and practiced.
The chat spikes with questions about seeds and maintenance.
Volunteers begin taking their cues, hands steady.
try to negotiate a pause
You draft a short, direct message to Lila—no accusation, only request.
Your fingers tremble over send; the message goes.
The group chat lights with urgent support, some angry, some hopeful.
The choices you offer are small, flavor-tinted—what kind of momentum you want to build in the next sixty minutes.
The action doesn't change the arc, but it shapes your breath.
Communities Build.
Hands Protect.
Names of neighborhoods ring the margins.
At dusk a drone hovers low—Noah Ortega's office sends an emissary instead of a man.
A projection appears against a tarp, the face of an advisor advising you to 'consider the big picture.' The voice is clipped, familiar but distant.
Advisor You risk the timeline for a patchwork of localized installations.
The Consortium can speed protections; we need uniform coherence.
Uniform protection that ignores people is a map without a heart.
Advisor We will not tolerate illegal occupation.
